narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your academic record: your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your family income level. The most influential was your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish average, which strongly signaled to the model a risk of struggling on the bar, pushing the prediction heavily toward failure. Nearly as important was your LSAT score of 25, far under the dataset average of about 37, reinforcing concerns about your readiness for the rigors ahead and further elevating the failure odds. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income stood out as a helpful factor, nudging the prediction slightly toward success compared to lower-income peers, but it wasn't enough to offset the academic red flags. Overall, your profile ranks in the lower deciles for grades and test scores relative to admitted students who typically pass the bar, placing you outside the favorable zone. While being a full-time student aligns with the vast majority who succeed, it didn't sway the balance here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—it's the model highlighting patterns from historical data where similar academic starts led to bar failures. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your undergraduate GPA if possible through post-bac courses, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and stable background. Building a stronger narrative around your Year 3 performance, perhaps with explanations or recent improvements, could also help in appeals or re-applications. With targeted preparation, many students in your position turn things around and succeed.
